title: case and default clauses should avoid containing lexical declarations
description: "case" and "default" clauses should not contain lexical declarationsAnalyze your codeconsistency-conventionalmaintainabilityCode SmellWhy is this an issue?More InfoThe ECMAScript specification allows for creating block-level lexical declarations (let,const,function, andclass) in any block statement or expression. However, when these declarations are made inside thecaseordefaultclause of aswitchstatement, they are not confined to the block of thatcaseordefaultclause. Instead, they apply to the wholeswitchblock but only get initialized when the cases are reached, which can lead to unexpected behavior.switch (foo) { case 1: let x = 1; // Noncompliant break; case 2: const y = 2; // Noncompliant break; case 3: function f() {} // Noncompliant break; case 4: class C {} // Noncompliant break; }To
recommendation: Replace with the recommended alternative approach.

checks.message: 'let' declaration found in case clause without block scoping - wrap in { } braces
'const' declaration found in case clause without block scoping - wrap in { } braces
function declaration found in case clause without block scoping - wrap in { } braces
class declaration found in case clause without block scoping - wrap in { } braces
lexical declaration found in default clause without block scoping - wrap in { } braces
